coredata.dc:title: Dispersant promotes mild formation of aminated-carbon quantum dots nanofiltration membranes for high-efficient resource recovery
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Dan-Dan
Fang, Rong
Wang, Long
Wang, Yue
Cao, Xue-Li
Liu, Mei-Ling
Li, Xianghong
Sun, Shi-Peng
coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterial-based membranes are a research hot spot for their sub-nanometer structural precision that can realize selective separation within a molecular scale. However, their preparation needs complex processes (heat treatment, time-consuming, vacuum-assisted filtration, etc), restricting their development. For the first time, the dispersant-poly (maleic acid-co-acrylic acid) sodium salt (PMAS) was designed to promote the mild formation of amine-carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) membrane at room temperature via a facile interfacial polymerization. First, PMAS incorporation may form an electric double layer and strengthen the electrostatic force between CQDs-NH2 and substrate, accelerating CQDs-NH2 particle deposition. Second, the PMAS may be conjunct with the adjacent CQDs-NH2 particles, lowing the long-range force, and improving CQDs-NH2 particle deposition behavior. Third, its surfactant property may regulate the interface compatibility, improving the reactivity to promote the mild preparation of the separation layer. The prepared membrane possessed high water permeance (55 L/m2·h1·bar1) and high selective separation to the NaCl/reactive black 5 mixture, showing great potential for wastewater treatment and resource recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial separation membrane
Amine-carbon quantum dots
Dispersant
Poly (maleic acid-co-acrylic acid) sodium salt
Mild preparation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membranes
Formation of CQDs-NH2 membranes (CxTy) without polyelectrolyte
Effects of PMAS incorporation on P-CxT1 membrane structure and surface properties
Possible mechanisms of PMAS mediated P-CxT1 membranes

Yue Wang thanks the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_1342).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of the CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membrane via IP method.
The effect of the CQDs-NH2 concentrations and TMC reaction time on membrane-formation properties with the same CQDs-NH2 immersion time (2 min).
Influence of CQDs-NH2 concentrations on the CxTy membrane-formation property with the same TMC reaction time (5 min).
The influence of the PMAS incorporation on the P-CxTy membrane-formation property (Na2SO4, 1000 ppm).
SEM images of membrane surface prepared at different conditions. (a) different CQDs-NH2 immersion times; (b) CxT1; (c) different CQDs-NH2/PMAS mixture immersion times; (d) P-CxT1.
AFM images of membrane surface prepared at different conditions after the IP process.
The WCA of membrane surfaces at different conditions.
Zeta potentials of membranes prepared at different conditions (a) Cx; (b) P-Cx; (c) CxT1 and (d) P-CxT1 membranes.
The N element XPS spectrum analysis of the P-CxT1 membranes. (a) P-C2.5T1; (b) P-C5T1; (c) P-C7.5T1; (d) P-C10T1.
The possible process for (a) pure CQDs-NH2, (b) CQDs-NH2/PMAS mixture deposition, (c) adsorption bridging mechanism between CQDs-NH2 and PMAS.
(a) The rejection of single solutes of P-C5T1 membrane and (b) long-term separation performance to NaCl/RB5 mixed solution.
Surface element analysis of the P-CxT1 membranes.
The distribution analysis of N-containing groups components.
The comprehensive evaluation with the references (preparation temperature, process time, and dye/salt separation performance).

row.entry: eriochrome black T (461.4 g·mol−1, EBT) 99.1%, NaCl 7.7%
two rounds of IP (DABA 30 min, TPC 15 min; PIP 10 min, TPC 5 min)
ME/NaOH 6 min; stored in DI water at 4 °C

head.ce:title: Dispersant promotes mild formation of aminated-carbon quantum dots nanofiltration membranes for high-efficient resource recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, National Engineering Research Center for Special Separation Membranes, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
National Engineering Research Center for Special Separation Membranes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, National Engineering Research Center for Special Separation Membranes, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming 650224, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming 650224, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterial-based membranes are a research hot spot for their sub-nanometer structural precision that can realize selective separation within a molecular scale. However, their preparation needs complex processes (heat treatment, time-consuming, vacuum-assisted filtration, etc), restricting their development. For the first time, the dispersant-poly (maleic acid-co-acrylic acid) sodium salt (PMAS) was designed to promote the mild formation of amine-carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) membrane at room temperature via a facile interfacial polymerization. First, PMAS incorporation may form an electric double layer and strengthen the electrostatic force between CQDs-NH2 and substrate, accelerating CQDs-NH2 particle deposition. Second, the PMAS may be conjunct with the adjacent CQDs-NH2 particles, lowing the long-range force, and improving CQDs-NH2 particle deposition behavior. Third, its surfactant property may regulate the interface compatibility, improving the reactivity to promote the mild preparation of the separation layer. The prepared membrane possessed high water permeance (55 L/m2·h1·bar1) and high selective separation to the NaCl/reactive black 5 mixture, showing great potential for wastewater treatment and resource recovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Million tons of waste salts are generated annually in the industries of agrochemical production, oil & gas development, wastewater treatment, etc. [1–3]. Realizing the utilization of waste salts makes a deep influence on the target of global carbon neutrality [4–6]. Nanofiltration (NF) membrane may realize precise molecular separation under size exclusion (special pore size distribution 0.5–2 nm) and Donnan effect (charged surface property), showing great potential for resource recovery and utilization [7,8]. The current commercial NF membranes are mostly thin-film composite (TFC) membranes prepared via the interfacial polymerization (IP) method. The reaction is finished within a few seconds [9–11]. It is difficult to control the preparation process in such a short time and customize the membrane separation property towards various demanding fields [12–14].

Researchers started to study thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, intending to control the membrane performance by incorporating nanoparticles [15–18]. However, large sizes of the nanomaterials and poor compatibility between nanomaterials and thin IP layers tend to lead to the selective layer showing poor membrane performance [19,20]. Besides, they may show poor structure stability when running in harsh conditions. The nanomaterial-based membranes are considered to be the ideal next-generation membrane for their excellent chemical stability and easy-structure-controllability property [21,22]. But for the nanomaterials-based membranes, their fabrication process needs to resolve the following challenges: (1) The long-range force (long van der Waals force and long electricity force) existence among particles with diameter ranges from 1 to 100 nm inhibits the particle gathering to form an intact distribution [23–25]. (2) The nanoparticle may aggregate and form irregular nanopores among the adjacent particles leading to poor membrane performance [26]. (3) The poor intermolecular force between the nanoparticles and polymer membranes leads to complicated preparation processes [27–29]. Previous reports showed that it may need 24–72 h to prepare defect-free zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) membranes or covalent organic frameworks (COFs) membranes at room temperature with the assistance of a modulator or adhesive [30,31]. Most of the nanomaterial-based separation membranes need external assistance (vacuum or pressure-assisted filtration, adhesion, coating, etc), restricting their development and practical applications [32,33].

Recently, carbon quantum dots-based organic nanomaterials are standing out for their good compatibility and dispersibility [34–36]. Our group reported a series of sandwiched nanofiltration membranes, proving that the CQDs may absorb to the polyethersulfone substrate firmly by simple immersion [37,38]. Matsuyama et al. fabricated the CQDs separation membranes successfully with the condition of 70 °C heat-treating [39]. However, preparing the defect-free CQDs membranes at room temperature efficiently remains unresolved. Strengthening the CQDs particle intermolecular force and improving the deposition behavior between CQDs and substrate simultaneously may be the key factors in the mild preparation of CQDs membranes effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the CQDs-NH2 as the only aqueous monomer, two types of CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membranes were prepared via the facile IP method (Fig. 1). The CQDs-NH2 solution was dissolved in DI water to form a homogeneous aqueous solution. The PES substrates were immersed into the diluted CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution for a certain time, reacting with the TMC in hexane afterward to form the CQDs-NH2 separation membranes, named as CxTy. The C and T refer to the membranes immersed by CQDs-NH2 or TMC, and x and y mean the immersion time, separately. The PMAS (0.2 wt%) was added into the CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution to regulate the formation of the CxTy membranes, named as P-CxTy.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800) was used to observe the CQDs-NH2 deposition on the PES substrates and the membrane morphologies. An atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension® Icon, Bruker) was used to observe the roughness of membranes. All the membranes were freeze-dried and spray-coated for 20 s with gold nanoparticles before testing. Attenuated total reflectance fourier transform spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet8700) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab250Xi) were used to test the variations of the surface chemical group and elements compositions separately. Zeta potential (SurPASS, Anton Paar, Austria) was used to test the membrane surface charge property changes within the pH ranging from 3.0 to 10.0 to analyze the CQDs-NH2 deposition behavior and separation performance. The membrane surface wettability with different coating conditions was tested by a water contact angle (WCA, Drop Meter A100P).

The membrane nanofiltration performance was tested in a lab-made filtration setup. The membrane was subjected to a 30 min pre-test at 6 bar to ensure the membrane was operated in a steady state [44]. The permeance (P, L/m2·h1·bar1) was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
P =
V
Δ P × S × t
where V is the permeate volume (L) collected at a certain time t (h), ΔP is the applied trans-membrane pressure (bar) and S is the effective membrane area (m2). The membrane retention property (R, %) was tested by different feed solutions (salts and dyes), which was calculated by Eq. (2):
(2)
R =
1 -
C p
C f
× 100 %
where C p and C f represent the permeate and feed concentrations separately. The salts and dyes concentrations were measured by a conductivity meter (FE30, Mettler-Toledo) and double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (A560, AOE, China) separately.

The separation factor S of the salt/dye mixture is defined as follows:
(3)
S =
R
dye
R
salt

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of CQDs-NH2 membranes (CxTy) without polyelectrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The TMC was designed to react with CQDs-NH2 to form the separation layer. The amount of CQDs-NH2 particle deposition and the reaction degree between CQDs-NH2 and TMC may affect the membrane permeance directly. Hence, the influence of CQDs-NH2 deposition amounts on membrane permeance was first investigated via changing aqueous concentrations and immersion time. Based on our previous study, the 2 wt% CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution was chosen as the initial concentration for experiments [37,38]. Taking the CQDs-NH2 immersion time for 2 min, we tested the membrane performance prepared with the conditions of different CQDs-NH2 concentrations and interfacial polymerization time. The MgSO4 possesses a larger hydration size and moderate charge properties than Na2SO4, MgCl2, and NaCl (Table S2). Therefore, the MgSO4 was chosen to test the membrane separation performance at the initial experimental stage (Fig. 2 ). The membrane performance decreased with the CQDs-NH2 concentration and immersion time increasing, which may be rationalized by the CQDs-NH2 particle deposition amount increasing.

Therefore, taking the IP reaction time for 5 min, the membrane performance prepared with the conditions of different CQDs-NH2 concentrations and immersion time was tested and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . With the CQDs-NH2 concentration increasing (from 2 wt% to 10 wt%) and immersion time (abscissa) prolonging, more particles may deposit on the membrane surface, which blocking the membrane pores and offering more CQDs-NH2 to react with TMC. Hence, the membrane permeance drops dramatically first. However, the membrane permeance tends to show steady permeance with the immersion time prolonging when the membrane was prepared with 7 wt% CQDs-NH2. The CxTy membrane showed stable performance gradually with the conditions of high concentration, long immersion, and reaction. It may be rationalizable to deduce that the membrane may form an intact selective layer after the membrane surface possesses enough CQDs-NH2 particles along with enough reaction time. The stable performance showed after long immersion and reaction time may be rationalized by the CQDs-NH2 concentration absorbed on the membrane surface achieving an equilibrium state with that of the aqueous solution above the membrane surface. The membrane developed to show low permeance with higher concentration or long immersion time, indicating the weak and slow deposition efficiency of the CQDs-NH2. It may be rationalized by the weak bond force (π-π stacking and electrostatic force) between the positively charged CQDs-NH2 and negatively charged PES substrate. Therefore, the membrane surface is possible to form a relatively intact separation layer after a long-time deposition of the CQDs. The experiments (Fig. 2 &3) prove that the low CQDs-NH2 deposition speed, amounts, and weak -NH2 reactivity co-conclude to poor membrane-formation properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of PMAS incorporation on P-CxT1 membrane structure and surface properties
ce:section.ce:para: The PMAS addition changed the membrane morphology and promoted the deposition of more particles on the membrane surface. Considering the hydrophilicity of CQDs-NH2, the WCA was used to evaluate its effects on membrane hydrophilicity (Fig. 7 ). With the aqueous immersion time changing from 2.5 to 10 min, the WCA of the membrane decreased from 61.4° to 53.23° and then up to 60.4°. The WCA should decrease due to the deposition of hydrophilic CQDs-NH2 on the membrane surface with increasing immersion time. The WCA was mainly affected by surface hydrophilicity and roughness. Considering the circa degrees of 61.4° and 60.4°, we dedicate that the decreased WCA value (55.57° and 53.23°) were caused by the rough surfaces of the membranes (Fig. 5 &6). After the IP reaction, all membranes showed similar WCA, which may be rationalized by the Marangoni process that the amine reacts with the TMC and form relatively similar morphologies and roughness (Fig. 5 &6).

The addition of PMAS changed the CQDs-NH2 deposition behavior (Fig. 5) and improved the membrane separation performance (Fig. 3 &4). Considering the charged properties of CQDs-NH2, PES substrate, and PMAS, the surficial property of membranes with different aqueous solution immersions were tested by zeta potential to analyze the effects brought by PMAS incorporation (Fig. 8 a&b). The pristine PES substrate showed a negatively charged surface. With the CQDs-NH2 immersion time increasing, the surface charge property of the Cx membranes was enhanced and the isoelectric point was increased from 5.22 to 5.33 due to the electric neutralization between CQDs-NH2 and PES substrate (Fig. 8a). After the PMAS was introduced, the P-Cx membrane positive charge property was further enhanced with the isoelectric point increased from 6.16 to 6.32 (Fig. 8b). The polycarboxylate-PMAS and PES substrates were both negatively charged. Hence, the increased positive charged property may be ascribed to the increase of the CQDs-NH2 amounts, i.e., the addition of PMAS induced more CQDs-NH2 particles deposition on the PES substrate surface. The -NH2 of CQDs-NH2 may react with -COCl of TMC during the IP reaction, leading to the –NH2 decrease and -COOH increase. Hence, the CxT1 (-36.80 mV) and P-CxT1 (-42.92 mV) membranes showed enhanced negatively charged properties compared with Cx (-19.83 mV) and P-Cx (-13.34 mV) membranes (Fig. 8a-d). The P-CxT1 membranes showed a higher negatively charged elevate degree (CxT1: -19.83 to -36.80 mV; P-CxT1: -13.34 to -42.92 mV). It means that the addition of PMAS promotes more TMC molecules combining with CQDs-NH2 and accelerates the reaction between CQDs-NH2 and TMC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanisms of PMAS mediated P-CxT1 membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the series analysis of the membrane structure and performance changes, we analyzed the possible mechanisms of CQDs-NH2 deposition behavior before and after PMAS incorporation. Taking pure CQDs-NH2 as deposition solution, the positively charged CQDs-NH2 with 4–6 nm diameters may adsorb slowly on the negatively charged PES substrate surface under long van der Waals force and electrostatic interaction (Fig. 10 a) [46]. The PMAS structures ( Figure S1 ) contain chain segments (hydrophobic alkyl chains) and anchoring segments (-COO– and Na+). Taking the mixture of CQDs-NH2 and PMAS as an aqueous solution, the PMAS may enter the PES substrate pores first and lower the substrate surface tension because its chain segments possess surfactant properties (Fig. 10b) [47]. Meanwhile, the Na+ may distribute towards the negatively charged PES substrate and the -COO– may towards the solution side, forming the electrical double layer and enhancing the electrostatic interaction between PES and CQDs-NH2 [48,49]. Furthermore, the PMAS may be conjunct with the adjacent distant CQDs-NH2 colloidal particles via adsorption and bridging due to the existence of anchoring segments (Fig. 10c) [50]. Therefore, more CQDs-NH2 particles may adsorb on the membrane surface in a short time, and the P-Cx membrane showed improved positively charged properties (Fig. 8). With the aqueous immersion time increasing, the CQDs-NH2 may subsequently occur multilayer adsorption due to the PES substrate mesopores structure (20–50 nm) [51]. The heterogeneous deposition roughens the membrane surface with diverse WCAs (P-C5, Fig. 7), resulting in a defective separation layer (P-C5T1). Along with the immersion time increasing, the adsorption tends to stop when the CQDs-NH2 concentration on the surface is equal to that of the upper solution, leading to the surface possessing a uniform CQDs-NH2 distribution. Hence, the membrane permeance keeps stable with enhanced selectivity when the IP reaction time is increased. The series of experiments proved that the PMAS addition improves the CQDs-NH2 deposition behavior, elevating the CQDs-NH2 membrane-forming efficiency.

This work reports a dispersant-mediated CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membrane via simple interfacial polymerization at room temperature. We demonstrated that (1) the slow CQDs-NH2 deposition and weak reactive activity induce the CQDs with poor membrane-forming properties. (2) The dispersant-PMAS accelerates the deposition and forms closely spaced nanoparticle distribution. Besides, its surfactant property may regulate the IP reaction and form a dense selective layer at room temperature. (3) The prepared CQDs-NH2 nanomaterial separation membrane exhibited stable permeance (∼17 L/m2·h1·bar1) with high separation efficiency to NaCl/RB5 in a long-term separation test, showing high potential in resource recovery. The study may provide new effective guidance to design nanomaterial separation membranes at room temperature with low cost and a simple preparation method.

Dan-Dan Shao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Rong Fang: Investigation, Validation. Long Wang: Investigation, Validation. Yue Wang: Resources, Supervision. Xue-Li Cao: . Mei-Ling Liu: Resources, Supervision. Xianghong Li: Resources, Supervision. Shi-Peng Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0211002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (32260367), Jiangsu Province Department of Science and Technology (BZ2022012), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (202201AT070463) jointly supported the work. Yue Wang thanks the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_1342).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.123759.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
